name: Ajax the Great
description: Ajax the Great, son of Telamon and Periboea, was a legendary Greek hero renowned for his immense strength, courage, and unwavering loyalty. Second only to Achilles among the Achaean warriors at Troy, Ajax was a towering figure known for his brute force and indomitable spirit. He distinguished himself in numerous battles, notably defending the Achaean ships from Hector's onslaught and fighting Hector to a draw in single combat. Ajax played a crucial role in the Trojan War, consistently proving his worth as a formidable warrior and a staunch defender of his comrades. After Achilles' death, Ajax contended for his armor, believing himself to be the rightful heir. When Odysseus was awarded the armor instead, Ajax was driven mad by Athena, leading him to slaughter livestock in a fit of rage. Upon regaining his sanity and realizing his dishonorable actions, Ajax, unable to bear the shame, committed suicide by falling on his own sword. His tragic fate serves as a cautionary tale about pride, honor, and the devastating consequences of unfulfilled expectations. Ajax's legacy continues to resonate as a symbol of unwavering strength and tragic heroism.
subtitle: Mighty warrior, tragic hero.
achievements: Defending the Achaean ships from Hector
Participating in numerous battles at Troy
Being considered second only to Achilles in strength

keyMyths.title: The Duel with Hector
keyMyths.description: Ajax volunteered to fight Hector in single combat to decide the outcome of a battle. The two warriors fought fiercely until nightfall, at which point the duel was declared a draw and they exchanged gifts in a show of respect.

keyMyths.title: The Contest for Achilles' Armor
keyMyths.description: After Achilles' death, Ajax and Odysseus both claimed the right to inherit his armor. A contest was held, and Odysseus was awarded the armor through trickery and Athena's intervention, leading to Ajax's madness.

keyMyths.title: Ajax's Suicide
keyMyths.description: Driven mad by Athena after losing the contest for Achilles' armor, Ajax slaughtered livestock, believing them to be his enemies. Upon regaining his sanity and realizing his shame, he committed suicide by falling on his own sword.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Epic poem detailing the events of the Trojan War, featuring Ajax as a prominent warrior.
sources.text: Epic poem detailing the events of the Trojan War, featuring Ajax as a prominent warrior.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: Tragedy focusing on Ajax's madness, shame, and eventual suicide.
